zones.label: Passive Buzzer
zones.summary: Membrane vibrates at the PWM frequency you set — plays any note
facts: Sound: membrane vibrates at the PWM frequency you set
Frequency range: ~20 Hz – 20 kHz; typical use 100 Hz – 10 kHz
Volume: low — approx. 60–70 dB at 10 cm
Tone: full melody — any note frequency is possible
Wiring: signal pin → buzzer → GND (2 connections total)
Power: driven directly from 3.3 V GPIO PWM pin
MicroPython: pwm = PWM(pin); pwm.freq(440); pwm.duty_u16(32768)
Best for: melodies, alerts, game sound effects, Morse code

zones.label: Active Buzzer
zones.summary: Internal oscillator makes one fixed beep tone — on/off only
facts: Sound: internal oscillator generates a fixed-frequency tone
Frequency range: fixed internal tone — typically ~2 kHz
Volume: medium–loud — approx. 70–85 dB at 10 cm
Tone: on/off only — single fixed beep tone
Wiring: VCC (5 V) → + pin; − pin → GND (or switch with transistor)
Power: 5 V recommended (louder than 3.3 V); uses ~30 mA
MicroPython: Pin(n, Pin.OUT).value(1) to beep; value(0) to stop
Best for: simple alarms, button-press beeps, low-code feedback

zones.label: Small Speaker + PWM
zones.summary: Voice coil driven by a PWM square wave — needs an amplifier
facts: Sound: voice coil moves cone — driven by PWM square wave from GPIO
Frequency range: 20 Hz – 20 kHz (wider range than a buzzer)
Volume: very low without an amplifier — needs transistor or op-amp
Tone: full melody with better tonal quality than passive buzzer
Wiring: GPIO → transistor base → speaker; collector to V+; emitter to GND
Power: PWM signal through NPN transistor (2N2222) or small amp IC
MicroPython: PWM(pin) — vary freq() for notes; can play simple tunes
Cost: $0.50–$2.00 speaker + ~$0.10 transistor
Best for: better-sounding tones; adding volume with a small amp IC

zones.label: I2S DAC + Amplifier
zones.summary: Digital audio decoded by a DAC and amplified — true music playback
facts: Sound: digital audio stream decoded by DAC, amplified to speaker
Frequency range: 20 Hz – 20 kHz; 24-bit depth at up to 96 kHz sample rate
Volume: loud — MAX98357A outputs up to 3.2 W into a 4 Ω speaker
Tone: full digital audio — WAV, PCM; tone synthesis, voice output
Wiring: 3 I2S bus pins (BCLK, LRCLK, DIN) + 5 V power + speaker
Power: 5 V supply to I2S amp module; I2S signal at 3.3 V
MicroPython: machine.I2S(id, sck, ws, sd, mode=I2S.TX, bits=16, rate=44100)
Cost: $3–$8 for MAX98357A breakout module + speaker
Best for: music playback, voice output, high-quality audio

quiz.question: Which method can play full music files like WAV and PCM with loud, high-quality sound?
quiz.explanation: An I2S DAC + amplifier (such as the MAX98357A) decodes a digital audio stream and amplifies it up to 3.2 W into a 4 Ω speaker — true music and voice playback, far beyond what a buzzer can do.

quiz.question: Which method can only make a single fixed beep tone using just Pin.value(1)?
quiz.explanation: An active buzzer has an internal oscillator that produces one fixed ~2 kHz tone. You just turn it on with Pin(n, Pin.OUT).value(1) and off with value(0) — no PWM and no choice of notes.

quiz.question: Which method plays any note with PWM, wires up with only 2 connections, and runs straight off a 3.3 V GPIO pin?
quiz.explanation: A passive buzzer's membrane vibrates at whatever PWM frequency you set, so it can play full melodies. It needs only a signal pin and GND and is driven directly from a 3.3 V GPIO PWM pin.

quiz.question: Which method needs an NPN transistor (like a 2N2222) before it makes a useful amount of sound?
quiz.explanation: A small 8 Ω speaker driven by PWM is very quiet on its own. Routing the GPIO signal through an NPN transistor (2N2222) or a small amp IC is what gives it useful volume.

quiz.question: Which method gives the loudest output, reaching up to 3.2 W into a 4 Ω speaker?
quiz.explanation: The MAX98357A I2S amplifier outputs up to 3.2 W into a 4 Ω speaker, making it the loudest option here — buzzers top out around 85 dB and a bare speaker is very quiet without an amp.
